ai_model_used: Advanced Rule-based Engine v2.0 (No API key — works offline)
property_issue_summary: The property inspection of Flat No. 103 (and associated areas) revealed widespread dampness and leakage across all major rooms — Hall, Bedrooms, Kitchen, Parking, and Common Bathrooms — primarily driven by systemic failure of bathroom tile grout joints and concealed plumbing. Thermal imaging confirmed active moisture retention across 30 readings, with all readings showing ΔT ~5°C signalling persistent moisture. The most critical finding is active slab leakage into the parking ceiling below, requiring immediate structural intervention.

area_observations.negative_side_description: Observed dampness at the skirting level of the Hall of Flat No. 103. The bottom 15–20cm of walls shows visible moisture staining and salt efflorescence deposits.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Gaps observed between tile joints of the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103, allowing water to seep through to the hall walls below.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: Skirting dampness covering the entire hall perimeter indicates widespread water ingress from the bathroom above. Efflorescence confirms prolonged exposure.

evidence.description: Observed dampness at the skirting level of the Hall of Flat No. 103. The bottom 15–20cm of walls shows visible moisture staining and salt efflorescence deposits.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: Failure of grout in Common Bathroom tile joints allowing water to percolate laterally and vertically into hall skirting walls.
recommended_actions: Re-grout all Common Bathroom tile joints with epoxy-based waterproof grout
Apply crystalline waterproofing slurry to hall skirting walls (internal surface)
Inspect and repair Nahani trap for any misalignment or cracks
Apply anti-dampness paint on hall skirting after drying
area_observations.chain_of_thought: Inspection shows skirting dampness → source identified as bathroom tile gaps → thermal confirms moisture retention in walls → HIGH severity due to extent

area_observations.negative_side_description: Dampness observed at the skirting level of the Common Bedroom of Flat No. 103. Similar pattern to the hall, affecting the shared wall with the Common Bathroom.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Tile joint gaps in Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103 are the common source for both hall and bedroom dampness, indicating widespread grout failure.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: Same bathroom affecting two rooms confirms large-scale waterproofing failure — not isolated to one joint.

evidence.description: Dampness observed at the skirting level of the Common Bedroom of Flat No. 103. Similar pattern to the hall, affecting the shared wall with the Common Bathroom.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: The same Common Bathroom tile joint gap failure causing hall dampness is migrating laterally into the bedroom wall cavity.
recommended_actions: Priority: Fix Common Bathroom grout as this resolves both hall and bedroom issues
Apply waterproof membrane on bedroom skirting wall (negative side treatment)
Hack and re-plaster affected skirting area after waterproofing
area_observations.chain_of_thought: Hall and bedroom both share Common Bathroom as source → single fix resolves both → confidence high due to clear spatial relationship

area_observations.negative_side_description: Dampness and efflorescence observed both at skirting level and on the wall surface of the Master Bedroom of Flat No. 103. Efflorescence (salt deposits) indicates long-term chronic moisture exposure.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Tile joint gaps in Master Bedroom Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Possible secondary contribution from external wall crack near the bedroom.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: Efflorescence on wall surface (not just skirting) signals chronic long-term moisture — the problem has been present for months or more. Dual sources (bathroom + external) increase severity.

evidence.description: Dampness and efflorescence observed both at skirting level and on the wall surface of the Master Bedroom of Flat No. 103. Efflorescence (salt deposits) indicates long-term chronic moisture exposure.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: Primary: Master Bedroom Bathroom tile joint failure. Secondary: External wall crack near duct providing rainwater ingress. Both pathways converge in the master bedroom wall.
recommended_actions: Immediate: Seal external wall cracks with elastomeric crack filler
Re-grout Master Bedroom Bathroom floor and wall tiles completely
Remove efflorescence chemically and apply crystalline waterproofing coat
Repaint with anti-fungal, breathable paint after drying
area_observations.chain_of_thought: Efflorescence = chronic exposure → dual sources confirmed → higher confidence due to physical evidence of salt deposits

area_observations.negative_side_description: Dampness observed at the skirting level of the Kitchen of Flat No. 103. Contained to the kitchen-bathroom shared wall area.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Tile joint gaps in Master Bedroom Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Water is migrating laterally through the shared wall between the bathroom and kitchen.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: Kitchen dampness is secondary to the Master Bedroom Bathroom issue. It will resolve once the primary source is fixed. Limited area affected.

evidence.description: Dampness observed at the skirting level of the Kitchen of Flat No. 103. Contained to the kitchen-bathroom shared wall area.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: Lateral migration of water from Master Bedroom Bathroom through shared wall cavity into kitchen skirting.
recommended_actions: Fix Master Bedroom Bathroom grout as priority — kitchen dampness will reduce
Apply waterproof skirting treatment on kitchen-bathroom shared wall
Monitor kitchen skirting for 4 weeks after primary repair
area_observations.chain_of_thought: Limited photo evidence + secondary cause → Moderate severity. Dependent on primary fix.

area_observations.area_name: External Wall — Near Master Bedroom, Flat No. 103
area_observations.negative_side_description: Damp wall surface and efflorescence on the exterior-facing Master Bedroom wall, consistent with rainwater ingress through external wall defects.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Moderate cracks observed on external wall surface near Master Bedroom duct. Gaps around duct/pipe penetrations creating direct rainwater ingress pathways.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: External cracks and open duct gaps create a direct path for monsoon rainwater into the building fabric. Combined with internal bathroom leakage, the wall is under attack from both sides.

evidence.description: Damp wall surface and efflorescence on the exterior-facing Master Bedroom wall, consistent with rainwater ingress through external wall defects.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: Moderate cracks and unsealed duct penetrations on the external wall allow rainwater ingress. This combines with internal bathroom leakage to saturate the master bedroom wall.
recommended_actions: Seal all external wall cracks with elastomeric crack filler (PU-based)
Apply 2 coats elastomeric waterproof external paint (weather-coat)
Seal all duct/pipe penetrations with polyurethane sealant
Inspect and repair external plumbing pipe openings
area_observations.chain_of_thought: External + internal dual attack confirmed → moderate crack = clear ingress path → HIGH severity due to year-round rainwater risk

area_observations.negative_side_description: Active leakage observed on the parking area ceiling directly below Flat No. 103. Water marks, staining and dripping visible on the RCC slab ceiling.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Concealed plumbing leakage and open tile joints in Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103 above. Water has accumulated sufficiently to penetrate the full RCC slab.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: CRITICAL — Active water penetrating a structural RCC slab is a serious structural concern. It indicates the waterproofing layer under the bathroom has completely failed. Parking areas are occupied by vehicles and people — safety risk.

evidence.description: Active leakage observed on the parking area ceiling directly below Flat No. 103. Water marks, staining and dripping visible on the RCC slab ceiling.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: Complete failure of the waterproofing membrane (brickbat coba layer) under the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Concealed plumbing leakage adds to water load. Water has pooled sufficiently to breach the structural slab.
recommended_actions: IMMEDIATE: Engage structural engineer to assess RCC slab integrity
IMMEDIATE: Isolate parking area below until slab integrity confirmed
Fix all plumbing joint leakages in Flat No. 103 Common Bathroom
Break and re-lay bathroom floor with new waterproof membrane (APP/SBS)
Apply epoxy injection or crystalline waterproofing on parking slab from below
Install stainless steel drip channels on parking ceiling to redirect residual water
area_observations.chain_of_thought: Active slab leakage = water has breached structural element → highest evidence quality → CRITICAL with 0.95 confidence

area_observations.negative_side_description: Mild dampness observed on the ceiling of the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Ceiling shows moisture staining and mild seepage marks.
area_observations.positive_side_description: Open tile joints and outlet leakage in both Common and Master Bedroom Bathrooms of Flat No. 203 (floor above). Water from Flat 203 is seeping through the floor slab into Flat 103's bathroom ceiling.
area_observations.severity_reasoning: Moderate severity — mild dampness from above-flat source. Requires coordination between two separate flat owners. Not immediately structural but will worsen if Flat 203 is not rectified.

evidence.description: Mild dampness observed on the ceiling of the Common Bathroom of Flat No. 103. Ceiling shows moisture staining and mild seepage marks.
Thermal readings show avg ΔT=5.01°C consistent with moisture presence

area_observations.root_cause: Open tile joints and outlet leakage in bathrooms of Flat No. 203 (above) allowing water to seep through the floor slab into the ceiling of Flat No. 103.
recommended_actions: Formally notify Flat No. 203 owner/building management of the issue
Re-grout all tile joints in Flat 203 Common and Master Bedroom Bathrooms
Repair outlet leakage in Flat 203
Apply anti-damp paint on Flat 103 Common Bathroom ceiling after repairs above are complete
area_observations.chain_of_thought: Source is in different flat → coordination dependency → lower confidence (0.78) as access may not be available → Moderate severity

probable_root_causes: Systemic failure of tile grout joints in Common and Master Bedroom Bathrooms of Flat No. 103 — open joints allow water to percolate to all adjacent areas.
Damaged or degraded Nahani trap and brickbat coba waterproofing layer under bathroom tiles — original waterproofing membrane has failed.
Concealed plumbing joint leakages in bathroom pipes of Flat No. 103 — contributing to water load in walls and slab.
External wall cracks and unsealed duct penetrations near Master Bedroom — direct rainwater ingress channel.
Open tile joints and outlet leakage in Flat No. 203 (above) — causing cross-flat seepage into Flat No. 103 bathroom ceiling.
Absence of functional waterproof membrane under wet areas — indicates either original construction quality issue or age-related degradation.

severity_assessment.overall_reasoning: Multiple interconnected dampness and leakage issues across 7 areas, with active slab leakage in the parking area indicating water has breached the RCC slab — this constitutes a Critical structural risk.

Hall \u2014 Flat No. 103.reasoning: Skirting dampness covering the entire hall perimeter indicates widespread water ingress from the bathroom above. Efflorescence confirms prolonged exposure.

Common Bedroom \u2014 Flat No. 103.reasoning: Same bathroom affecting two rooms confirms large-scale waterproofing failure — not isolated to one joint.

Master Bedroom \u2014 Flat No. 103.reasoning: Efflorescence on wall surface (not just skirting) signals chronic long-term moisture — the problem has been present for months or more. Dual sources (bathroom + external) increase severity.

Kitchen \u2014 Flat No. 103.reasoning: Kitchen dampness is secondary to the Master Bedroom Bathroom issue. It will resolve once the primary source is fixed. Limited area affected.

External Wall \u2014 Near Master Bedroom.reasoning: External cracks and open duct gaps create a direct path for monsoon rainwater into the building fabric. Combined with internal bathroom leakage, the wall is under attack from both sides.

Parking Ceiling \u2014 Below Flat No. 10.reasoning: CRITICAL — Active water penetrating a structural RCC slab is a serious structural concern. It indicates the waterproofing layer under the bathroom has completely failed. Parking areas are occupied by vehicles and people — safety risk.

Common Bathroom Ceiling \u2014 Flat No..reasoning: Moderate severity — mild dampness from above-flat source. Requires coordination between two separate flat owners. Not immediately structural but will worsen if Flat 203 is not rectified.

🔴 IMMEDIATE: Stop all water flow — fix concealed plumbing joint leakages in Common Bathroom
🔴 IMMEDIATE: Replace damaged Nahani trap and restore brickbat coba waterproofing layer

🟠 SHORT-TERM: Formally notify Flat No. 203 management for bathroom repairs — documented in writing

🟡 MEDIUM-TERM: Anti-dampness treatment on Hall, Bedroom, and Kitchen skirting walls
🟡 MEDIUM-TERM: Remove efflorescence from Master Bedroom wall chemically and repaint
🟢 LONG-TERM: Commission full building waterproofing audit for all flats on the same floor

estimated_repair_urgency: Immediate (within 48hrs) for parking slab; Short-term (2 weeks) for bathroom waterproofing
additional_notes: No previous structural audit or repair work documented — this is a first inspection.
Emissivity set to 0.94 for all thermal readings (appropriate for painted plaster surfaces).
Leakage confirmed to occur at all times — not limited to monsoon season.
Leakage from Flat No. 203 (above) is affecting Flat No. 103 ceiling — cross-flat coordination required.
Concealed plumbing leakage confirmed via inspection checklist — likely contributing to all skirting dampness.
External wall shows moderate cracking near Master Bedroom duct — weather ingress pathway confirmed.

thermal_analysis_summary: Thermal inspection was conducted on 27/09/22 using a GTC 400 C Professional (Serial: 02700034772). A total of 30 thermal readings were captured across the property. Surface temperatures ranged from 20.1°C (coldspot) to 28.8°C (hotspot). The average temperature differential (ΔT) across all readings was 5.01°C, with a peak ΔT of 5.4°C recorded at Reading #1. A total of 30 readings were flagged as anomalous based on elevated temperature differentials (ΔT > 3°C threshold), strongly corroborating the inspection findings of active moisture ingress across the property.
